coredata.dc:title: Engineered full-color-emissive lignin carbon dots enable selectively fluorescent sensing of metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Xinyan
Xu, Jikun
Zhou, Pengfei
Dai, Lin
Zhang, Jiaming
Xiao, Xiao
Zhang, Chuntao
Huo, Kaifu
coredata.dc:description: As a burgeoning luminescent unit, multifaceted carbon dots (CDs) from ideally sustainable and aromatic-like lignin biopolymer have been igniting the renaissance to alleviate the bimodal anxieties of carbon neutrality and ecological environment. However, the dilemma of synthesizing full-color-emissive CDs with a modulated solvent-engineering is hugely lopsided which severely impedes the illumination of linking their photoluminescence mechanism and selective metal-ion-sensing functions. Herein, the solvent-engineered, lignin-derived multicolor CDs (blue, green, yellow, and red light) with a distinctively well-defined nanostructure consisting of sp 2/sp 3-hybridized carbon skeleton and partially surface-functionalized domains (e.g., –OCH3, –OH, –COOH, and N, S co-doping) are proposed to maneuver the molecular-structure-sensing correlations. We found that full-color N, S co-doped CDs with a narrow size distribution of 2.13–2.63 nm have the rapidly selective quenching abilities to fluorometrically probe the explicit metal-ion systems of Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+ and/or Al3+ under a wide response range (0–500 μM) with high accuracy. The factors of lignin molecular state, quantum size, zeta potential, electrostatic and cation-π interactions that endow their unique fluorescence, real-time and sensitive monitoring provoked by polar solvents are comprehensively highlighted. Hence the present attempt not only provides a scalable tactic to rationally nanoscale-design lignin valorization with a blueprint of expanding the biorefining toolbox, but also paves the avenue to forecast specific metal-ion recognition of multicolor CDs coordinating molecular structure and photophysical properties.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic system and fundamental properties of the full-color fluorescence emissive carbon dots (CDs) using technical lignin as the sole precursor. (a) A solvent-engineering strategy for preparing blue, green, yellow, and red fluorescent CDs (BCDs, GCDs, YCDs, and RCDs) from ethanol, acetone, ethyl acetate, and water respectively. (b) Quantitative functional groups (mmol/g) of hydroxyl (−OH) and carboxyl (−COOH) in lignin. CS: condensed syringyl unit. CG: condensed guaiacyl unit. Total P-OH: the total content of phenolic hydroxyl. (c) The weight- average (M w) and number-average (M n) molecular weights, and polydispersity index (PDI, M w/M n) of lignin. Basic structural linkages of lignin including (d) side-chain δC/δH 50–90/2.5–6.0 and (e) aromatic regions δC/δH 100–135/5.5–7.5 in the two-dimensional heteronuclear single quantum coherence nuclear magnetic resonance (2D HSQC NMR) spectrum. (f) Existent substructures of lignin identified in the 2D HSQC NMR spectra including β-aryl ether (A, β-O-4′ linkages with a free –OH at the γ-carbon), Cα-oxidized β-O-4′substructures (A′), resinol (B, formed by β–β′, α-O-γ′, and γ-O-α′linkages), phenylcoumaran (C, formed by β-5′and α-O-4′linkages), syringyl (S), ferulate (FA), guaiacyl, (G), and p-hydroxyphenyl units (H), free p-coumaric acid (pCA). (g) The potential scheme of the formation mechanism from technical lignin to multicolor CDs, mainly including oxidized cleavage/nitrogen doping and aromatic refusion/deoxygenation reactions.

Morphological characterizations of the full-color emissive lignin carbon dots (LCDs). (a) TEM images of BCDs, GCDs, YCDs, and RCDs (from left to right, scale bar 20 nm). Inset: the corresponding high-resolution TEM images for determining their lattice size d. (b) The lateral size distributions of BCDs, GCDs, YCDs, and RCDs (from left to right). (c) AFM images and corresponding height profiles of BCDs, GCDs, YCDs, and RCDs (from left to right, scale bar 400 nm).

Physicochemical properties of the full-color emissive LCDs. (a) FTIR spectra of BCDs, GCDs, YCDs, and RCDs through solvent engineering with ethanol, acetone, ethyl acetate and water, respectively. Full XPS survey scan (b), high-resolution XPS C 1 s (c), N 1 s (d), O 1 s (e), S 2p (f) spectra, and Raman spectrum (g) of the selected RCDs. The quantitative zeta potential values (h) of BCDs, GCDs, YCDs, and RCDs with a gradually-lowered negative charge.
Optical performance of the selected LCDs. The PL mapping images of BCDs (a), GCDs (b), YCDs (c), and RCDs (d) derived from technical lignin.

The selectivity of BCDs and GCDs. Relative fluorescence emission intensities of the BCDs (a) and GCDs (b) upon adding various kinds of metal ions. Changes in BCDs fluorescence intensity upon adding different concentrations (0 −500 μM) of Fe3+ (c) and Ag+ (e), as well as, the corresponding fitting plot of (F 0 −F)/F 0 versus concentration of Fe3+ (d) and Ag+ (f). Emission spectra of GCDs with varying Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 500 μM (g), and the fitting relationship between fluorescence quenching (F 0 −F)/F 0 and Fe3+ (h) (wherein F 0 represents the fluorescent intensities of original LCDs and F defines the various fluorescence intensities of mixed LCDs/metal-ions system).

The selective recognition of metal ions toward YCDs and RCDs. Relative fluorescence emission intensities of the YCDs (a) and RCDs (b) upon adding different kinds of metal ions. Changes in YCDs fluorescence intensity upon adding various concentrations (0 −500 μM) of Fe3+ (c) and Cu2+ (e), which correspond the fitting plot of (F 0 −F)/F 0 versus concentration of Fe3+ (d) and Cu2+ (f). Fluorescent emission spectra of RCDs with varying Fe3+ (g), Cu2+ (i), and Al3+ (k) concentrations from 0 to 500 μM, and the established relationship between fluorescence quenching (F 0 −F)/F 0 with Fe3+ (h), Cu2+ (j), and Al3+ (l) (wherein F 0 represents the fluorescent intensities of original LCDs and F discloses the various fluorescence intensities of mixed LCDs/metal-ions system). (m) The proposed molecular-structure-sensing correlations to direct the potential influence of macromolecular lignin state (e.g., heteroatoms doping, interlinked substructures, average molecular weights, and interfacial functional groups) on the LCDs structure (e.g., sp 2/sp 3 hybridization, quantum size, zeta potential, bulk band architecture, and energy transfer), thereby dominating their selective metal-ion identification and sensing properties (e.g., Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+, and Al3+) with specific fluorescence quenching mechanisms (e.g., electrostatic interaction, adsorbed molecular status, complexation, and cation-π correlation). CB: conduction band; VB: valence band.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a burgeoning luminescent unit, multifaceted carbon dots (CDs) from ideally sustainable and aromatic-like lignin biopolymer have been igniting the renaissance to alleviate the bimodal anxieties of carbon neutrality and ecological environment. However, the dilemma of synthesizing full-color-emissive CDs with a modulated solvent-engineering is hugely lopsided which severely impedes the illumination of linking their photoluminescence mechanism and selective metal-ion-sensing functions. Herein, the solvent-engineered, lignin-derived multicolor CDs (blue, green, yellow, and red light) with a distinctively well-defined nanostructure consisting of sp 2/sp 3-hybridized carbon skeleton and partially surface-functionalized domains (e.g., –OCH3, –OH, –COOH, and N, S co-doping) are proposed to maneuver the molecular-structure-sensing correlations. We found that full-color N, S co-doped CDs with a narrow size distribution of 2.13–2.63 nm have the rapidly selective quenching abilities to fluorometrically probe the explicit metal-ion systems of Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+ and/or Al3+ under a wide response range (0–500 μM) with high accuracy. The factors of lignin molecular state, quantum size, zeta potential, electrostatic and cation-π interactions that endow their unique fluorescence, real-time and sensitive monitoring provoked by polar solvents are comprehensively highlighted. Hence the present attempt not only provides a scalable tactic to rationally nanoscale-design lignin valorization with a blueprint of expanding the biorefining toolbox, but also paves the avenue to forecast specific metal-ion recognition of multicolor CDs coordinating molecular structure and photophysical properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the thriving movement of carbon neutrality, plant biomass that is the sustainable, bulk, and low-cost resource in nature has great promise to function as the multifaceted alternatives of petrochemical energies and materials (Chen et al., 2021; Dimitrakellis et al., 2022; Droguet et al., 2022). As one of the lignocellulose building blocks up to 15–40 wt%, lignin is generally produced from the oxidative polymerization of p-hydroxycinnamyl alcohol monolignols with a various degree of methoxylation originating from the aryl-ether and C−C linked phenylpropanoid pathway (Figueirêdo et al., 2022; Neutelings, 2011). As a byproduct, technical lignin is large-scale manufactured from papermaking and biorefining factories with the annual output of approximately 60–70 Mt (Balakshin et al., 2021; Ragauskas et al., 2014). Over the past several decades, the urgent renaissance of upvaluing lignin has been commonly comprehended in the fundamental researches and industrial communities (Sun et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2022). From a chemical standpoint, a family of substructures (e.g., guaiacyl, syringyl and p-hydroxyphenyl moieties) with interunit bonds are produced to impel lignification, including β-O-4′ (β-aryl ether), β-5′ (phenylcoumarans), β-β′ (resinols), 5–5′ (dibenzodioxocin), β-1′ (spirodienones), and 4-O-5′ (diaryl ether) linkages (Sun et al., 2020). These chemical substructures endow lignin with giant potentialities to function as an indispensable puzzle for bio-based carbonaceous materials, arenes or jet-fuels (Bertella and Luterbacher, 2020; Figueiredo et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2018). Moreover, the well-developed polyaromatic lignin with inherent chromophores also could clutch the key to incorporating and boosting colorful photo physicochemical properties (Li et al., 2022, 2021).

Due to the superior optical/surface functionalization and biocompatibility, nanoscale carbon dots (CDs) that are designated by characteristic sizes of < 10 nm have attracted intensive concerns over a newly emerged motif of luminescent nanomaterials during the last decade (Jiang et al., 2020a). Hence they are emblazing the renovations in the manifold domains of biomedicine, optoelectronics, and sensing, supposing that these CDs could be tailor-made to fluoresce upon a wide-range of practically available wavelengths (Ðorđević et al., 2022; Mocci et al., 2022). It should be emphasized that the awkward situation with CDs is the feasibility and tunability of entire full-spectrum emission from blue to red, which also need to synthesize through using only one set of precursors in a scalable fashion (Wang et al., 2021a, 2020). It thus is of crucial essence that the optimal CDs could be rationally fabricated via picking the ideal feedstock and solvent engineering (Jiang et al., 2015). The good news is that the natural lignin with high carbon content ignites the helpful wildfire of synthesizing full-color CDs due to its graphite-like heteropical phenylpropanoid interunits with alterable functional groups and molecular weights, thereby affording a golden circumstance of installing their carbonaceous skeleton (e.g., sp 2/sp 3 hybridization) into CDs with crystallized structure and surface modification (e.g., edge and intrinsic defects) (Zhou et al., 2022). Nevertheless, the early products of lignin-derived carbon dots (LCDs) are still largely incipient with the bottleneck in emitting the finite fluorescence color (blue or green) (Ding et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2022). And uncertainties in full-color-emissive mechanism not only confuses the steerable performance but also constrains the practical application of LCDs (Zhu et al., 2021), which is of an ongoing conundrum to be conquered for low-cost LCDs with the whole visible color range. Practically feasible, low-cost, point-of-care fluorescence CDs nanoprobes that provide the rapid and accurate responses are crucial for tracing metal-ions to minimize water pollution and prevent detriments at the outset (Bhamore et al., 2018; Kailasa et al., 2019). One of the essential challenges in lignocellulose-based CDs is to acquire their entire spectrum emission with the possible selectivity of multiple metal-ions detection by controlling the well-defined biopolymer structure. Appropriate biomass precursors and functionalized protocols, without consuming the optical, chemical and/or biological properties, are indispensable to fulfill the improved selectivity and sensitivity of full-color CDs toward metal-ions. Taking advantages of lignin structure and solvent engineering, the characteristics of LCDs that includes nano-size, surface functionalities, edge structure, multicolor emission, and morphologies can be facilely tuned to maneuver the metal-ion selectivity.

In the work described herein, we delineate a facile two-step strategy of acidic oxidation cleavage followed by solvent engineering to acquire tunable LCDs that irradiate remarkably full-color-fluorescence emission from blue to red by using technical lignin as the precursor. The in-depth studies affirm that the nanoparticle size, zeta potential, and photoluminescence (PL) wavelength of LCDs over a broad-spectrum range can be readily modulated by switching the reaction solvents. To grasp fundamental insights into the luminescent mechanism, the trends for structural characters and functional moieties of lignin and its LCDs are connected to efficiently navigate their molecule-structure-PL sensing relationships. The pith of this research is stimulated by the foresight of R. Buckminster Fuller, who said: “There is no energy crisis, just a crisis of ignorance.” (Wang et al., 2022b) This is the underpinning of the well-developed full-color LCDs from lignin waste in pulp and bioethanol tailings, which generalizes a brand-new lifestyle transferring lignin from a harmful grave to a sustainable cradle. Hence the “waste-treats-pollutant” conception that upgrades lignin into full-color LCDs with the selective capabilities of metal-ion identification and fluorometric sensing are highlighted.

Technical lignin is kindly provided by the Shandong Longlive Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Jinan City, China). Herein, the concise schedules of separating industrial lignin are given as below: the corncob biomass was hydrothermally dilute sulfuric acid treated to dissociate hemicelluloses followed by the second-step extraction with aqueous sodium hydroxide; thereafter, the alkaline filtrate was regulated using sulfuric acid into acidic environment with a pH value of ∼ 2 for detaching the precipitated lignin as the crude feedstock. Throughout the whole experiment, the chemicals are of analytic grade (e.g., ethanol, acetone, and ethyl acetate) and the constant resistivity of deionized water are controlled to approximately 18 MΩ·cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvothermal synthesis of full-color-spectrum fluorescent lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: A brief scheme for the synthesis of lignin-derived full-color CDs is depicted in the Fig. 1a. First, Alkali lignin of 10 g was dispersed in 50 mL of ultrapure water. Second, the concentrated nitric acid of 15 mL was dropped into the mixed solution slowly under continuous stirring to control the reaction temperature. After ultrasonic treatment for 4 h, the resulting suspension was diluted and then filtrated through a 0.22 µm filter membrane. Third, the as-prepared samples were exhaustively rinsed by deionized water to recover the HNO3-treated lignin. During a typical process, the HNO3-oxidized lignin of 3 g was uniformly dissolved in 90 mL individual solvent (i.e., ethanol, acetone, ethyl acetate or H2O). Using a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, the resultant mixture was solvothermally reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. To eliminate the unreacted lignin oligomers, the products of corresponding LCDs were filtrated through the organic membranes and then dialyzed with the molecular weight cut-off 3000 Da. Ethanol, acetone, ethyl acetate, and water were chosen to perform the solvothermal procedure of preparing full-color LCDs, which determined the unique fluorescence behavior of LCDs corresponding with the blue (BCDs), green (GCDs), yellow (YCDs), and red (RCDs) emission respectively. In the end, the physicochemical properties of lignin-to-LCDs and their fluorescent sensing capabilities are gauged via an array of advanced instruments (supporting information). All of the experiments about quantifying the metal-ion contents or fluorescent intensities (e.g., Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+ and/or Al3+) are performed in triplicate and the results are presented as the average values with the standard deviations less than 5 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fundamental structure of lignin and its conversion to carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Aspired with illustrating the relationship between lignin structures, LCDs properties, and metal-ion sensing, it thus is highly logical to sketch the contours of scaling-up lignin valorization in the material spheres of nanoparticle and environment. The schematic diagram to facilely synthesis full-color-emissive CDs from lignin through a two-step procedure, including HNO3-aided ultrasonication followed by solvothermal treatment in an individual reaction system, is clearly demonstrated in Fig. 1a. The oxidized lignin fragments with N-doped functional groups can be further aromatically refused into BCDs, GCDs, YCDs and RCDs through solvothermal engineering of ethanol, acetone, ethyl acetate and water, respectively. After dialysis and freeze-drying, the brown powder of LCDs that holds a highly stable water-solubility is obtained with a single yield of ca. 18 % on the basis of lignin weight. The quantum yields (QY) of the as-prepared LCDs in aqueous solution are approximately 8–9 %. It should be mentioned that the QY of RCDs is slightly lower than that of BCDs, GCDs and YCDs, which is ascribed to the fact that the luminescent center might be destroyed with the presence of extreme carbonization.

Of Note, the discrepancies in composition, interior linkages, and reactivity of lignin subunits could trigger a cascade expression of carbonaceous scaffold and surface functionality. As a naturally aromatic macromolecules, the unique structure of lignin includes phenylpropanoid subunits (e.g., guaiacyl G, syringyl S, and p-hydroxyphenyl units H) and immanently functional groups (e.g., −OH, −COOH, and –OCH3) that directly related with the decoration of sp 2/sp 3 hybridization and intrinsic/edge defects into CDs. In the meanwhile, the average molecular weight for lignin precursor is significant to decide the nanoparticle-size and molecular status of LCDs. Therefore, elucidating the well-defined lignin structure that strongly links the physicochemical features and fluorescent sensing performance of nanocarbons is adopted as an essential prerequisite of lignin-to-CDs. It has witnessed an extensive uptake of the quantitative 31P NMR technique that functions as an efficient mode of evaluating the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in lignin. As can be seen from Fig. 1b, the contents of phenolic OH (total P-OH), COOH, and aliphatic OH armoring with lignin molecules are quantified to be 2.7472, 1.6211, and 1.609 mmol g−1, respectively. In general, the cleavage of β-aryl ether linkages produces phenolic OH groups, while oxidative reactions would trigger the lignin fragmentation with the concomitant formation of carboxyl (Wen et al., 2015). This suggested that the depolymerization of natural lignin has taken place with the potential of incorporating more active sites (e.g., COOH and P-OH) into LCDs. The main species of phenolic hydroxyl appears to be guaiacyl OH (G-OH, 1.1231 mmol g−1), followed by syringyl OH (S-OH, 0.6234 mmol g−1) and p-hydroxyphenyl OH (H-OH, 0.5933 mmol g−1). The contents of condensed moieties that includes CG-OH and CS-OH are 0.2389 and 0.1684 mmol g−1 respectively, further explaining the partial lignin condensation. As a function of molecular weights, the distributed phenolic groups from heterogeneous lignin structures would be directly connected to the surface functionality of LCDs that are partly responsible for their photophysical performance. As summarized in Fig. 1c, the weight-average (M w) and number-average (M n) molecular weights of alkali lignin are 2660 and 785 g mol−1 respectively, which codetermines a limit in the boundaries and hybridization of carbon cores. The calculated polydispersity index (PDI, M w/M n) represents the discrepant or gradient distribution of molecular lignin particles that hold the ability to generate multicolor LCDs with diverse nanosizes and optics.

The 2D HSQC NMR characterization is conducted to check the beginning-to-end message from the compositional and structural items of lignin (e.g., representative linkages and subunits). The structural assignment of 1H–13C cross-signals corresponding to the cardinal lignin motifs are presented in Table S1. The branched chains with main inter-unit bonds of lignin can be observed in the aliphatic region between δC/δH 50–90/2.5–6.0 (Fig. 1d). Accompanying by the β − β′ (B, resinol) and β − 5′ (C, phenylcoumaran) linkages, the isolated lignin is readily found to be rich in β-aryl ether substructures (β-O-4′, A) (Wang et al., 2022c). It was disclosed that the crystallized structure of lignin-based carbons is well-linear correlated with lignin β-O-4′ linkages (Li et al., 2021). Accordingly, the rational design of chemical processing to fabricate high-quality LCDs can be tuned under the navigation from such a well-defined relationship with lignin molecular structures. Through boosting the revenues from lignin nanocarbons, hence the cellulose-leading bioeconomics of both biorefining and papermaking industries could be renovated over a directional manipulation of high β-O-4′ bonds (Li et al., 2021). From the branches, the specific S/G lignin aromatics are easily interacted with the methoxyl that is likely conducive to generate an edge defect structure via solvothermal demethoxylation. The inserted carbohydrates in the lignin side-chain that also affect the sp 3 hybridization and crystalline structure of LCDs can be differentiated upon the location of C–H correlations in β-D-xylopyranoside from the aliphatic region of lignin. As presented in Fig. 1e, the lignin aromatic domain ranging from δC/δH 100–150/6.0–8.0 refers to the fundamental phenylpropanoid substructures. The cross-signals of lignin motifs that originate from guaiacyl (G), syringyl (S), ferulate (FA), and p-hydroxyphenyl (H) are explicitly discerned (Shen et al., 2020). The prominent signals of p-coumaric acid (pCA) that is always acylated at C-γ of herbaceous lignin side-chain bring a straight-forward evidence to the leftover of pCA under the moderate alkaline extraction (Wen et al., 2015). The lignin-based cardinal motifs corresponding to the 1H–13C cross-signals are easily annotated in Fig. 1 f. More interestingly, the lignin oxidation of inherent G and/or S sub-units might be taken place to produce their deuterogenic acids functioning as aromatic chromophores, which are then in-situ confined by fatty acids via hydrogen-bonding to trigger intensified spin-orbit coupling and afterglow emission (Wan et al., 2022). Accordingly, the oxidative lignin fragments offer a chance to promote the molecular fluorescence of LCDs via adhering onto their surface.

By all accounts, the possible formation mechanism of lignin-derived CDs that involves acid dissociation of technical lignin followed by the subsequent aromatic refusion of lignin intermediates is depicted in Fig. 1g. It is generally appreciated that the main β-O-4′ ether linkages capture approximately 60–70 % of the total lignin bonds. In detail, the HNO3-oxidized cleavage of alkali lignin generates in-situ sulfur-introduced lignin segments via breaking aryl-ether bonds (e.g., β-O-4′ and α-O-4′) and/or C−C linkages (e.g., β–5′), along with N dopants. Of note, it can form several new kinds of active sites including oxygen-, nitrogen- and sulfur-functional groups. During the pretreatment, hence the nitric acid personates a tri-functional member of nitrating/oxidizing agent, “top-down” scissor, and additional dopants to produce multiple lignin intermediate molecules, which thus function as carbon precursors for the solvent-mediated synthesis of full-color LCDs. Eventually, heterogeneous lignin fragments are aromatically refused via the increased sp 2 hybridization and the prompt π-π stacking under the solvothermal treatment. The enhanced C–S, C–N, and C–SOx groups are synchronously taken place with respect to the deoxygenation/carbonization processing. Owing to the discrepancy in lignin segments, the solvent polarity-tuned reaction provokes the alterable physicochemical properties of LCDs that differ greatly in full-color fluorescence emissions responsible for multiple radiative recombination paths back to the ground state (Zhu et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of multicolor luminescent lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: It is highly recognized that the physicochemical features of CDs dominate their ultimately optical and sensing performances (Jia et al., 2022). We thus utilize the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) to examine the morphology and size of four typical LCDs. In Fig. 2a and S2, the TEM images exhibit that the as-prepared BCDs, GCDs, YCDs and RCDs belong to the quasi-spherical and evenly monodispersed nanodots. At the macro level, their amorphous structures with inhomogeneous shapes and sizes can be partially discriminated in the TEM images. From the insets of Fig. 2a, the high-resolution TEM profiles illuminate the typical carbon nuclei and highly-crystallized nature of LCDs with analogously well-resolved lattice fringes. The crystal interplanar d-spacings of 0.21 and 0.24 nm link with the (100) and (1120) planes of graphite respectively, which is ascribed to the differences in heterogeneous lignin fragments and solvent polarity (Bhattacharya et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2019; Zhan et al., 2018). Their average transverse sizes are approximately 2.13, 2.34, 2.39 and 2.63 nm for BCDs, GCDs, YCDs and RCDs, respectively (Fig. 2b). The quantitative TEM results highly suggest that the red-shift fluorescence of LCDs is correlated to the quantum confinement with an incremental nanosize, which narrows band-gap as a consequence of p electron delocalization (Wang et al., 2021a). Besides the size effect, the sp 2/sp 3 hybridized domains have a bearing on luminescence. Generally speaking, the emissive wavelength of CDs should not be red-shifted if there is no sp 2 hybridization. With respect to LCDs, the native sp 2 phenyl substructure of lignin provides a favorable foundation for inserting the sp 2 hybridization into the carbon frameworks. In the case of CDs with alike nanosize, the enhanced ratio of internal sp 2 hybridization triggers the red-shift emission, disclosing that the fluorescent LCDs highly depends on both the size and the sp 2/sp 3 hybridized domains (Wang et al., 2021a). Possible aggregation of the as-prepared LCDs is investigated by AFM. The topographic altitudes of the as-prepared LCDs mostly range from ∼0.3–2 nm in Fig. 2c, illuminating that the LCDs comprise of single to triple layers of graphite (Ding et al., 2018).

The elemental compositions and chemical structures of the typical LCDs are estimated through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As indicated in Fig. 3a, all of these LCDs possess the similar stretching vibration bands except for slight intensity variations, such as −OH/NH at ∼3300–3550 cm−1, carboxylic CO at ∼1680 cm−1, sp 2 CC at ∼1460 cm− 1, and C−OH at ∼1215 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2020). Their coexistence deciphers that the four LCDs samples are readily surface-functionalized by weak electron-withdrawing −COOH groups and forceful electron-donating –OH/NH2 groups, originating from the intrinsic groups of lignin and the subsequent HNO3-oxidization. Additionally, these hydrophilic groups endow their excellent solubility in water. The stretching vibrations of C−N and C−C bonds are also identified for these LCDs, which indicates the formation of polyaromatic structures in their carbon cores during the reaction procedure. The location of ∼900 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of C−S/CS with a profound discrepancy in its intensity, confirming that the YCDs are additionally functionalized by medium electron-withdrawing −SO3H and S−O related groups (Li et al., 2019). The FTIR results confirm that N and S-containing functional groups are successfully shaped and co-doped into the framework of LCDs via solvothermal carbonization. It is worth emphasizing that the assignable variations in surface functional groups (e.g., −OCH3, −OH, −COOH, −SO3H and −NH2) are of importance to differ the fluorescent sensing performances of multicolor LCDs. As a representative, the surface functionalization of oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur elements for the selected RCDs can also be disclosed by the full-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 3b), which exhibits four typical peaks of C1s (∼285 eV), N1s (∼400 eV), O1s (∼533 eV), and S2p (∼169 eV). As displayed in the fitting curve of Fig. 3c, the deconvolution of high-resolution C1s pattern into four well-resolved peaks are clearly located at 284.8 eV (CC/C−C), 286.1 eV (C−N), 286.9 eV (C−O), and 288.8 eV (CO), further suggesting that the oxygen-containing groups are locked at the interface of as-synthesized RCDs (Fan et al., 2022). The N 1 s signal are split into the components of –NO2 at 405.1 eV, N–H at 401.5 eV, N–(C)3 at 400.7 eV, and C–N–C at 399.2 eV (Ding et al., 2018). It thus is inferred that the nitrogen-dopants are compatibly decorated at the edge of LCDs with a striking preference over basal plane sites, which is ascribed to the highly-crystallized structure of the selected RCDs with small defects. The characteristic signals of CO, –NO2 and C−O is also distributed at 531.2, 532.8 and 533.5 eV from high-resolution O1s spectrum, respectively (Fig. 3e). The S2p profile (Fig. 3f) that can be well-fitted into three peaks at ∼168.1, 168.9, and 169.8 eV affirms the presence of C−S−C covalent bonds and C−SO x −C sulfone bridges within the as-prepared RCDs (Miao et al., 2017). Given all that, it is confident to delineate a fact that the LCDs are co-doped with N and S elements/groups (e.g., −OH, −COOH, −NH2, −SCN and C−SO x ) on their surface. The existence of hydrophilic groups is conducive to disperse LCDs in the aquatic medium, which might also be adopted as functional trapping groups for identifying and detecting metal-ions. As discerned in Fig. 3g of the Raman spectrum, we can easily locate two intense signals at 1581 and 1371 cm−1 that correspond to the graphite (G band) and defects (D band) conditions, respectively (Liang et al., 2021). Ordinarily the presence of G-band is usually accepted to mirror the sp 2 atomic vibration modes, while the D-band defines the sp 3 hybridization with atomic lattice deficiency. Howbeit there are no obvious D and G bands for BCDs, GCDs and YCDs. During the Raman characterization, the extremely strong fluorescence irradiations of BCDs, GCDs and YCDs provoke the interference to the shape of their D, G-bands, which leads to the atypical spectra with the absence of legible D and G peaks (Wang et al., 2022a). In our case, the intensity ratio of D to G bands (I D/I G) is quantified to be 0.68 (< 1), which manifests the high graphitic configuration of RCDs. And for the species of RCDs, the limited number of defects with the presence of graphitic nitrogen atoms in the original sp 2 domains hold the contribution to its red-shifted luminescence. The surface charge of CDs, which in turn can deliver reasonable information to pinpointing their functional groups as well as the dispersibility in suitable media, has been studied via quantifying zeta potential (ζ). Through measuring the ζ values of LCDs in the aqueous solution, it indicates that the discrepancies in the species and contents of surface groups leads to a gradually-reduced negative charge from − 30.575 (RCDs) to − 2.48 mV (BCDs) corresponding with the blue-shift emission. From this inference, the charge intensities of LCDs form a close alignment with their unique chelation ability of metal-ions. Last but not least, the luminescent variations are attributed to the difference in the particle size, oxidation degree, sp 2/sp 3 hybridization, and N, S, O-interrelated surface/ζ potential states of LCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing performance of full-color-emissive lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of full-color LCDs shed a direct insight into their selectively photoluminescence sensing performance of metal ions. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra of the as-fabricated LCDs present the similarly excitonic absorbance over the wavelength ranging from 200 nm to 350 nm, which predominantly results from π − π * transition of graphite sp 2 domain and n − π * transition of CO/CN (Liu et al., 2021; Miao et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2013). The normalized photoluminescence (PL) spectra of LCDs possess the specific PL peaks at approximately 450 nm (BCDs), 490 nm (GCDs), 530 nm (YCDs) and 620 nm (RCDs) under the excitation of adsorbed wavelength, respectively (Fig. S3). The 3D fluorescence excitation-emission mapping ( Fig. 4) of LCDs are also plotted to clearly present the comparison between these four samples. A family of solvents is set up to plot the solvation effect during the synthesis processing of the LCDs, which successfully modulated their multicolor ranging from blue to red with an ordered sequence. In this vein, the sizes of LCDs are increased gradually from 2.13 to 2.63 nm, inferring that the presence of quantum size dominates the tunable PL wavelength with a red-shifted manner. The fluorescent emission spectra of LCDs are displayed in Fig. S4 by varying the excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 590 nm with a 10 nm interval. Due to the action of quantum confinement, the LCDs present an excitation-stimulated emission that is a familiar profile of carbon nanodots due to their highly graphited structure. Despite the emission wavelength of all LCDs red-shifts more or less with the incremental excitation wavelength, the narrow variations of emission generally belongs to excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. The optical phenomenon of full-color LCDs modulated by single solvothermal system also anticipates that the reaction solvent with low or high polarity leads to a controllable migration of LCDs fluorescence properties. Of note, the excitation-dependent phenomenon could be traced to incorporating oxygen, nitrogen and/or sulfur functional moieties into LCDs.

Inappropriate levels of metal ions always bring serious detriments to water environment and human health. As an emerging, efficient and real-time nanoprobe, the fluorescent CDs usually contain multifunctional groups that have excellent affinities with metal ions (Chen et al., 2020; Dong et al., 2022). However, the structure-performance relationship between CDs and targeted metal ions is ambiguous which impedes the credible prediction of specific ions species and/or concentrations. In virtue of the full-color emission LCDs with tunable structure, it can be expected that their selective recognition and sensing of metal ions can be actualized via the tailor-made LCDs with unique metal-chelating abilities. In this connection, the linear or exponential functions between the concentrations of recognized metal ions (μM) and the variation of fluorescent intensities (F 0 −F)/F 0 are fitted, wherein F 0 represents the fluorescent intensities of original LCDs and F defines the various fluorescence intensities of mixed LCDs/metal-ions system. As usual, we adopt the LCDs with an identical dosage to forecast their acceptable selectivity of different metal ions. Diverse metal ions that includes Co2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, Ba2+, K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Ni2 +, Ag+ and Mn2+ are appended into the aqueous LCDs solution with a fixed concentration of 1000 μM. To examine their responsive ability, hence the PL spectra under an optimal excitation wavelength are collected. Encouragingly, the individual change of PL intensities deciphers that the as-synthesized LCDs exhibit their own selectivity to lock metal ions (e.g., Fe3+, Ag+, Al3+ and/or Cu2+). Taking advantage of varying ionic concentration over a wide range from 0 to 500 μM, the fluorescence quenching titration is carried out to authenticate a fact that the LCDs possess a similar behavior of dose-dependent response. In general, a weakened LCDs fluorescence is riding on the gradually increased ion-dosage, while the responding intervals and sensitivities are varied with different color of LCDs. Besides, the competing test was conducted by adding 500 μM specific Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+ or Al3+ to the aqueous solution of LCDs in the presence of 500 μM other metal-ions. As expected, a negligible difference in the fluorescence intensity of LCDs is determined after mixing with any of interferential metal-ions. The complicated sensing mechanisms within designing a fluorescence nanoprobe, such as Förster/fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), coordination induced aggregation, PL quenching, have been declared on account of either fluorescent attenuation or intensification (Torres Landa et al., 2022). Biomass-related precursors are widely used to produce fluorescent CDs for detecting metal-ions (Torres Landa et al., 2022). The PL quenching can be accepted to the electron transfer between metal-ions with carboxyl/hydroxyl groups present around the CDs, which results in the coordination-induced aggregation of CDs. The dynamic PL quenching mainly involves the transfer of electrons from the excited state of highly luminescent CDs to the vacant orbital of metal-ions, resulting in non-radiative electron-hole recombination (Torres Landa et al., 2022).

Over the past decade, several CDs derived from lignocellulose have been adopted to probing metal-ions in water (Jiang et al., 2020b; Torres Landa et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2021b), such as lotus root for Hg2+, stem of banana plant for Fe3+, bamboo leaves for Cu2+, coriander leaves for Fe3+, peanut shells for Cu2+, papaya waste for Cr3+/Cr6+, biorefinery residue consisting of sugars and depolymerized lignin for Fe3+, hemicellulose for Ag+ (Table S2). From Fig. 5a, the fluorescent quenching degree of both Fe3+ and Ag+ about BCDs exceed 90 %, endowing their high Fe3+/Ag+ affinities with specific sensitivity. With respect to GCDs, the mono-species of Fe3+ appears a remarkable quenching effect (∼74 %) on their luminescence (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the BCDs deliver a fluorescent sensing ability of Fe3+ ions in a low concentration from 0 to 50 μM with a ultrahigh linear coefficient of R 2 0.99952. An exponential function (R 2 =0.98387) is established when further elevating Fe3+ concentration of 50–500 μM. On the other side (Fig. 5e and f), a linear fitting curve is assigned with the narrow Ag+ concentration ranging from 0 to 40 μM via percent quenching (R 2 =0.96325). As for the GCDs, the fluorescence intensities decrease with the increase of Fe3+ concentration under the separated function regions from 0 to 100 μM (linear R 2 =0.99432) and 100–500 μM (exponential R 2 =0.9634) respectively (Fig. 5g and h). The unique is that the YCDs enable a bimodal recognized performance with Fe3+ and Cu2+, exhibiting the quenching degree of 85 % and 38 % respectively ( Fig. 6a). Surprisingly, the unparalleled tri-functional identification of Fe3+, Cu2+ and Al3 + is achieved for the RCDs with the high quenching efficiencies (Fig. 6b). In the case of YCDs suspended in water, the standard curves of sensing Fe3+ (Fig. 6c and d), an exponential function of 0 − 100 μM and a linear correction of 100 − 500 μM are both fitted with the good coefficients (R 2 >0.99). Besides a linear reduction in percent quenching is observed upon adding 30–110 μM of Cu2+ ions with a high R 2 = 0.994 (Fig. 6e and f). Based on the RCDs, there exists an excellent linear relationships between the fluorescent intensity with Fe3+ (R 2 0.992)/Cu2+ (R 2 0.997) concentration in the ranges of 50–100 μM/40–90 μM respectively (Fig. 6g-j). It is delightful to achieve that (Fig. 6k-l) the Al3+ linear fitting intervals are readily split into low- and high-concentration with a confirmable boundary of 100 μM, corresponding to the calculated coefficient R 2 of 0.996 and 0.987 respectively.

The tunable surface conditions of full-color LCDs possess the diversified affinities of metal ions with variable radius wherein the nitrogen or sulfur forms a stable non-fluorescent complex, thereby resulting in the distinctive sensitivity and selectivity for metal ions based on the fluorescent quenching of the selected LCDs (Liu et al., 2019; Torres Landa et al., 2022). In the same instant the metal ions might bind to multiple heteroatoms (i.e., N, S and O) of the LCDs to ignite complexation, which thus modifies their electronic structure of the LCDs and finally lead to non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation responsible for aggregate-induced quenching (Guo et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2022). In this regard, how to manipulate the molecular properties of lignin and its LCDs are the linchpin of interpreting the molecule-structure-PL sensing interplay that provides a general guide to their controllable synthesis with a specially appointed ion-recognition. Due to the unique phenylpropanoid substructure of naturally heterogeneous lignin biopolymers, herein the photophysical features of their derivative LCDs can be systematically regulated by facilely varying the composition of reaction solvents, thereby enabling the fluorescent switching from blue to red. As summarized in Fig. 6m, the proposed correlations to direct the potential influence of macromolecular lignin state (e.g., heteroatoms doping, interlinked substructures, average molecular weights, and interfacial functional groups) on the LCDs structure (e.g., sp 2/sp 3 hybridization, quantum size, zeta potential, bulk band architecture, and energy transfer), thereby dominating their selective metal-ion identification and fluorometric sensing properties (e.g., Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+, and/or Al3+) with specific fluorescence quenching mechanisms. In more detail, the well-designed lignin structure includes aromatic subunits (e.g., S, G and H) and aliphatic branches (e.g., C−C bonds) corresponding to the sp 2 domains and sp 2 networks respectively, which directly pilot the crystalline and defect engineering. Another fact of lignin involves its molecular weights/polydispersity, functional motifs (e.g., −OCH3, −COOH and −OH), and heteroatom doping (e.g., N, S and O) that can be quantified to assess the functionalized degree, nanosize and zeta potentials of LCDs. The PL quenching phenomenon of LCDs are widely accepted by means of the surface functionalized moieties which coordinate with metal-ions in turn to serve the selective and sensitive metal-ion sensing. It is also of significance that molecular state luminescence from the structured lignin fragments, which are similar with organic luminescent molecules, carries the responsibility to mediate the fluorescent emission of LCDs. Herein accompanied by the gradual increase of nanoparticle size (2.13–2.63 nm) and negative ζ potential (−30.575∼−2.48 mV), the bulk band structure of LCDs would be changed subsequently with the decreased band-gaps from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) that are highly correlated with the peculiar metal-ion PL quenching through the coupling behaviors of electrostatic interaction, adsorbed molecular aggregates, metal-chelating and cation-π correlation. Last but not least, the well-designed full-color LCDs from tailor-made lignin with a facilely solvent-switchable system could potentially benefit the specific recognition of metal ions, which is of huge importance to the dual territories of water-environmental monitoring and biomass conversion.

Grave-to-cradle upvaluing of lignin biomass that carries an exceptionally high CO2 footprint is mandated world-wide from the biorefining and/or papermaking industries. Instead of deeming lignin as a huge waste, the multipronged chemical and/or material technologies could valorize it into a sustainable and structured biopolymer resource. Herein the full-color LCDs that emit stable and bright fluorescence ranging from blue, green, yellow to red light are fabricated with the oxidized/well-defined lignin via easily tuning the reaction solvent (i.e., ethanol, acetone, ethyl acetate and H2O) for the ratiometric detection of specific metal ions (e.g., Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+ and/or Al3+). Specifically, the sharply multicolor emission of LCDs that originates from the quantum size, sp 2/sp 3 hybridization and surface functionality with the aid of ordinary solvent engineering can navigate their selective recognition and responsive sensitivity of multiple metal-ions. The functions of exponential and linear models with high coefficients (R 2 0.963 −0.999) between ionic concentrations and fluorescence quenching degrees (1 −F/F 0) of LCDs upon appending manifold metal-ions are operated to promote the prediction of PL sensing performances. Accompanied by the gradual increase of nanoparticle size (2.13–2.63 nm) and negative ζ potential (−30.575∼−2.48 mV), the luminescent and physicochemical structures of LCDs would be tuned to practice the metal-ion PL quenching through the coupling behaviors of electrostatic interaction, adsorbed molecular aggregates, metal-chelating, and cation-π correlation. An unique fact is that the water-mediated RCDs enable a trimodule sensing ability with Fe3+, Cu2+ and Al3+, which are of great concern to scaling-up in the future biorefining technologies. Benefiting from the well-designed full-color emission and fluorescence turn-off model, this sensing system is selective, sensitive and economic with the tailor-made lignin carbon nanodots that elaborates a shining story of expanding biomass valorization.

Xinyan Hou: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jikun Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing-original draft, Writing-review & editing. Pengfei Zhou: Writing-review & editing. Lin Dai: Writing-review & editing. Jiaming Zhang: Data curation, Writing-review & editing. Xiao Xiao: Data curation, Writing-review & editing, Resources. Chuntao Zhang: Writing-review & editing. Kaifu Huo: Resources, Project administration.
